title: Chapter 13: The Truth
content: The battery charger was warm against Jane's ribs, tucked inside her cardigan like contraband. She'd found it in the supply closet that afternoon — a bulky portable unit meant for the ancient ThinkPad Bertha kept in the basement, the one Grace mentioned only in passing, the one that hadn't been charged in months because nobody thought it mattered. Nobody thought Bertha mattered.

Jane had timed Grace's rounds to the second. 11:47 PM — the corridor check. 12:04 AM — the kitchen. 12:15 AM — the stairwell to the upper floors, which meant the basement door was unwatched until 12:22. A seventeen-minute window. She had her alibi laid out in advance: a note on her desk saying she'd be in the archive room cataloging swatches until late, a thermos of coffee grown cold beside it, the lamp left burning. If anyone came looking, the scene would tell the story of a diligent archivist working past midnight.
The basement door opened without a sound. Jane had oiled the hinges that morning.

She descended. The stairs were concrete, unadorned, the air dropping three degrees with every step until her breath hung in small clouds. The corridor at the bottom was dark except for the amber crack of light beneath Bertha's door.
Jane pushed it open.

Bertha was standing by the entrance. Not at the worktable, not at the window that wasn't a window — right there, inches from where the door swung inward, as though she'd been standing in that exact position since Jane left twenty-four hours ago. As though the intervening day had been nothing but a held breath.
"You came back."
Jane stepped inside and closed the door behind her. "Did you think I wouldn't?"

The silence that followed was its own kind of answer. Bertha's fingers found Jane's wrist — cold, deliberate, the touch of someone verifying that the body in front of her was real and not another draught of medicated dreaming. Her grip was stronger than expected, the joints pronounced, a seamstress's hands.
"Everyone doesn't," Bertha said. "Eventually."
The loneliness in that sentence was a wound. Not self-pity — something worse. The flat certainty of someone stating a weather pattern. Rain falls. People leave. The sky is blue.
Jane pulled the battery charger from her cardigan and held it out. The warmth of it bled through the fabric between them. "For the laptop."

Bertha stared at it. Her expression didn't change, but her grip on Jane's wrist tightened for a fraction of a second before releasing. She took the charger, turned it over in her hands like an artifact from a country she'd forgotten existed, and set it on the worktable beside the dormant ThinkPad.
"Sit down," Bertha said. It wasn't a request.

Jane sat on the single wooden chair. Bertha remained standing, unfolding a canvas stool from beneath the table and positioning herself across from Jane, their knees almost touching in the cramped space. The room smelled of fabric sizing and lavender and something else — something chemical and faintly antiseptic that Jane couldn't place. It was coming from Bertha's left sleeve.
"You want to know how I got here," Bertha said. Not a question.
"I want to know the truth."

Bertha laughed. It was short and dry, like the bark of a dog that had forgotten how to bark for joy. "The truth. All right. The truth is that Edward Rochester recruited me from design school in Paris. I was twenty-two. Top of my year at the Chambre Syndicale. He came to the graduate show — sat in the front row, the way he does, like he's measuring the fabric off the models instead of looking at the design. Afterward he found me. Told me my silhouette work was the most original thing he'd seen in a decade. Offered me a position on the spot."

She held up her hands, turned them over, studied them. "He was right. My silhouette work was extraordinary. I knew it. He knew it. That was the trap — being seen accurately by someone who understood the value of what he was seeing."
"Tell me about the beginning," Jane said.

"The beginning was real." Bertha's voice shifted, softened at its edges. "He didn't steal my work. Not at first. We built Thornfield together — my vision, his capital. He'd bring me fabrics, I'd transform them. He'd tell me about the market, I'd tell him about the drape. It was a collaboration. A real one. I was young and I was in love and I was flattered, and those three things together are the most dangerous combination a woman can hold."
"What changed?"

"His name went on the building." Bertha said it simply. "And eventually, that became the same as his name on the work. The first season, we were co-creators. The second, I was his head designer. The third, I was his employee. By the fifth —" She spread her hands. "I was his secret. The invisible hand. You've seen the press. 'Edward Rochester's singular vision.' His singular vision. That phrase alone should have been a warning. Singular. As if there were no others in the room."

The chemical smell intensified as Bertha shifted, reaching for a glass of water on the worktable. Jane tracked the motion and caught it — the edge of a bandage at Bertha's inner elbow, the faded bruise blooming beneath the gauze like a watercolor in green and yellow. An injection site.
"How did you find out about the supply chain?" Jane asked.

Bertha's mouth twisted. Not a smile. Something sharper. "That's the grotesque irony. I found it because I was trying to help. The spring collection — three years ago — I wanted a transparency feature. You know the trend? Provenance mapping. QR codes on the labels that trace each garment back to its source. Sustainable, honest, modern. I designed the whole campaign. Gorgeous work. The kind of thing that wins awards and changes industry standards." She paused. "So I traced the fabric myself. I wanted the chain to be real, not just marketed. I started with our linen supplier in Tours, followed the subcontractor to Tunis, then to Lagos, then to —" She stopped. Her jaw tightened.
"What did you find?"

"Children. Toxic chemicals in the dye vats — chromium, formaldehyde, azo compounds that are banned in the EU but miraculously appear in our supply chain through a shell company registered in Malta. Workers without protective equipment hand-dipping fabric in carcinogenic baths. And the shell companies — five of them, nested like Russian dolls, each one making the money trail disappear before it reaches Thornfield's clean, beautiful, award-winning ledgers."

Jane felt something cold settle in her stomach. She'd suspected — God, she'd suspected since the first time she saw those ledgers that didn't quite add up — but suspicion was abstract. This was architecture.

"I confronted him," Bertha continued. "Right here, in this room. I had the documents. I had the photographs from the Lagos facility. I had the shell company registrations. I laid it all out on this table and I said, 'Edward, we have to fix this. We have to go public, recall the line, change the suppliers.' And do you know what he did?"
"Denied it?"

"He looked at me like I was a child asking where rain comes from." Bertha's voice went flat, precise, reciting. "He said: 'This IS the business, Bertha. You think honesty is free? Someone pays for every organic label. The question is who.' He didn't deny a single thing. He just didn't see it as wrong. It was math. It was logistics. The suffering was an input, like thread count or yardage."
Jane's hands had curled into fists in her lap. She unclenched them deliberately.

"I told him I was leaving," Bertha said. "That I would go to the press. That I would take my portfolio and my name and walk out the door and never come back." She touched the bandage on her arm again — an absent, habitual gesture. "He refused to let me leave."
The room seemed to contract around them. The amber light from the single lamp threw shadows up the walls like inmates pacing a cell.

"He had a psychiatrist," Bertha said. "On retainer. A man who owed him a considerable professional favor — I learned later that Edward had quietly funded his private clinic when the banks wouldn't. So when Edward called and said his wife was having episodes, that she was a danger to herself, that she needed evaluation — the doctor came. He asked questions. I answered them honestly, because I had nothing to hide."

She met Jane's eyes. "I have bipolar II. I've had it since I was nineteen. Diagnosed, treated, managed — lithium, therapy, regular sleep. I've never had a psychotic episode in my life. But by the time Dr. Fournier finished his report, I had bipolar II with psychotic features. Intractable. Treatment-resistant. Dangerous to myself and others. Court-ordered commitment. Mandatory medication."
The words landed like verdicts.

"He didn't break the law, Jane." Bertha's voice was steady, almost clinical. "He used it. He weaponized my own diagnosis. The bipolar is real — I've managed it for years. He just... exaggerated. Added features. Made the medication mandatory. Made the commitment involuntary. Every six months there's a review, and every six months Dr. Fournier submits the same report, and every six months the court extends the order. It's all legal. Every stamp, every signature, every injection."

She pulled back her sleeve fully, exposing the bandage and the constellation of bruises beneath it — old ones in mustard and violet, fresh ones in red-black, the chronicle of two years written on her skin. The chemical smell was sharper now. Antiseptic. Whatever they were injecting her with, it was administered with clinical precision and hygienic indifference.

"He sends Grace to give me the shots," Bertha said. "Grace believes the diagnosis. She thinks she's caring for a sick woman. She's not cruel — she's convinced. That's what makes it work. Nobody questions a nurse following a doctor's orders."
Jane's throat was tight. "How did you — how have you stayed—"
"Working?" Bertha stood. "Come. I'll show you."
She crossed to the far wall, the one Jane had glimpsed before but never fully examined. Bertha pulled back a curtain of hanging muslin, and the room doubled.

It was a workshop. A real one — not the cramped corner Jane had seen on her first visit, but a hidden atelier stretching back beneath the east wing of the house. Garments hung from a pipe rack along the wall: dresses, coats, a silk blazer with such precise seaming that Jane's breath caught. Sketches were pinned to a corkboard in layered constellations — hundreds of them, years of designs, each one dated in Bertha's small meticulous hand. Bolts of fabric were stacked on industrial shelving. A dress form stood in the corner, dressed in a half-finished gown that seemed to emit light — copper silk with an overlay of black chantilly lace, the kind of piece that would stop a runway show cold.

"My hands keep moving," Bertha said quietly. She was standing among her work like a woman in a garden she'd planted from seed. "Even drugged. Even caged. That's the one thing he couldn't take from me. I make things. It's how I stayed sane. It's how I stayed *me*."

She turned. In the low light, her face was stripped of pretense, raw as exposed wire. "I wasn't the first, you know. There were others before me. Designers, assistants, interns. He finds talented women and he — absorbs them. Takes their best work, reshapes them into what he needs, then discards them. A pattern cutter from Lyon. A textile artist from Mumbai. An intern from the Sorbonne who lasted four months before she simply disappeared from the industry. I hear she works in a bakery now. She was brilliant, Jane. Brilliant and broken."

Bertha's hand lingered on the copper gown. "I'm just the one who survived. Because I refused to stop working. Because my hands kept moving even when my mind was —" She gestured vaguely. "Elsewhere. And because he still needs me. My designs still anchor every collection. He can't lose me without losing the engine that runs Thornfield. So he keeps me. Down here. Useful and invisible."

Jane looked at the racks of garments, the years of secret work, the woman standing among them like a curator in her own museum of survival. She saw Bertha — fully, for the first time. Not the feral creature of the corridors, not the madwoman, not the ghost. A woman who had turned her prison into an atelier. Who had made art inside a cage. Who had been unseen for so long that being seen felt like violence and salvation in the same breath.

Recognition passed between them like a current. Bertha's eyes glistened, but she did not cry. She straightened her spine and lifted her chin, and in that gesture Jane understood: self-pity was a luxury this woman could not afford. Self-awareness was the weapon she had instead.

"Jane." Bertha's voice was different now. Urgent. Clear. "He's doing the same thing to you. The gifts. The wardrobe. The way he looks at you like you're a raw material — something to be cut and shaped and worn."
Jane held her gaze. "I know."
"Then what are you going to do about it?"

The question hung in the air between them. Jane didn't answer immediately. She looked around the room — the silk-lined cage, the brilliant garments arranged like votive offerings, the sketches that chronicled a woman's refusal to disappear. The warmth of the battery charger still lingered on her skin, a small practical act of solidarity. Bertha's cold fingers had branded her wrist. The chemical smell of the injection bandage sat at the back of her throat like a swallowed scream.

This was the truth. Not merely what Edward had done, but what he was — a system of absorption, a machine that turned women into material. And the machine was still running. It was running right now, overhead, in the beautiful house above them where the press clippings hung in silver frames and the organic certifications lined the foyer wall.
"We take him down," Jane said.
Bertha stared at her. "Just like that?"
"Just like that."

For one breath, two, something fierce and bright passed across Bertha's face — hope, maybe, or the memory of it. Then it shuttered. She looked at Jane with an expression that held no doubt about her conviction, only doubt about the world.
"He's not just a man, Jane." Her voice was low, measured, afraid. "He's a system. And systems don't fall because two women decide they should."

The silence that followed was not empty. It was full — full of every injection, every stolen design, every year in the dark, every garment that carried Edward Rochester's name and Bertha Mason's hands. It was full of the question that neither of them could answer yet, but that Jane could no longer avoid asking:
*Can we actually do this?*

The lamp flickered. Somewhere above them, floorboards creaked — Grace, probably, making her rounds, checking on the madwoman who was anything but mad. And in the basement atelier, surrounded by silk and steel and years of unbroken resolve, two women sat in the gap between what they wanted to believe and what they feared was true.
